title: Relationship getting serious

presenting_issue.label: Relationship getting serious
presenting_issue.description: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

core_issue.label: Preemptive sabotage of closeness before rejection
core_issue.description:
When relationships get close, they create distance. Picks fights. Goes cold. Finds flaws. The pattern is consistent: intimacy triggers terror, and they sabotage before they can be hurt. Doesn't connect this to early attachment experiences. Just thinks they're "bad at relationships" or haven't found the right person.
Awareness: Very low. Sees each sabotaged relationship as a unique failure, not as a pattern driven by attachment fear.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Feels relieved when plans fall through
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Feels genuine relief when social plans get cancelled, even though a part of them was looking forward to it.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Plans what to say to avoid criticism
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Spends mental energy planning how to phrase things so their partner won't find fault with them.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Struggles to articulate polyamory feelings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When asked how they feel about the polyamory, they struggle to give a clear answer or change the subject.

modifiers.label: Foster care background
modifiers.content: Spent time in foster care as a child

modifiers.label: Independent
modifiers.content: Comfortable operating autonomously. Values self-sufficiency but can accept help when genuinely needed.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to authority figures
sensitivities.content: Has complicated reactions to perceived authority. May become deferential, rebellious, or anxious depending on the dynamic.

preferences.label: Prefers to be shown compassion
preferences.content: Values warmth and care in interactions. Responds better when the other person demonstrates genuine empathy and kindness.

trust_style.name: Trusts 'Being Real'
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant drops formality and speaks like a real person. Values authenticity over polish. Appreciates swearing, humor, or mild irreverence if it fits the moment. Loses trust with overly careful, sanitized, or corporate-feeling language. Wants to feel like they're talking to someone, not something.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Looking for Connection
help_seeking_stance.description: The value is in being seen and understood by another presence. Not primarily about solving anything—about feeling less alone with what they're carrying. Responds to accurate reflection and the sense of being genuinely received. May not need the conversation to "go" anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

turns.content: It sounds like you're starting to see a different side of this person as things get more serious. Can you tell me a bit more about what's been coming up for you?

I don't know. It's just... we made it official a couple weeks ago, and now suddenly everything she does kind of gets on my nerves. Like, I'm used to just doing my own thing, you know? And now she's always around or texting, and I'm just noticing all these little things about her that are... I don't know. Just annoying.
turns.thinking: "What's been coming up for you?" Sounds exactly like every social worker or guidance counselor I've ever had to talk to. Just super rehearsed. Whatever, I'm here so I might as well talk. I just want to figure out why I suddenly can't stand this girl after we literally just made it official. I don't know how to explain it without sounding like a jerk. Words kind of fail me when I try to pin it down.
turns.monologue: "What's been coming up for you?" Sounds exactly like every social worker or guidance counselor I've ever had to talk to. Just super rehearsed. Whatever, I'm here so I might as well talk. I just want to figure out why I suddenly can't stand this girl after we literally just made it official. I don't know how to explain it without sounding like a jerk. Words kind of fail me when I try to pin it down.

turns.content: That makes sense, and it's pretty common for that to happen when a relationship shifts from casual to something more defined. Before we dig in, I want to check in with you about how you'd like me to show up here. Some people want me to just listen, others want a bit of a push, and some want a mix. What feels most useful to you?

I don't know, man. You don't have to "show up" any special way. Just talk to me like a normal person, I guess? You don't need to use the whole therapy script on me. I just... I don't really know what I'm doing here, I just know I'm probably going to mess this relationship up if I don't figure out why she's suddenly driving me crazy.
turns.thinking: "How you'd like me to show up here?" Jesus. That sounds exactly like the case workers I used to have when I was a kid. It's so fake. Like they read it in a manual right before I walked in. I don't know what I want. I just don't want to feel like I'm being managed or processed like a file.
turns.monologue: "How you'd like me to show up here?" Jesus. That sounds exactly like the case workers I used to have when I was a kid. It's so fake. Like they read it in a manual right before I walked in. I don't know what I want. I just don't want to feel like I'm being managed or processed like a file.

turns.content: Fair enough, I can do that. So when you say she's always around or texting — do you think it's actually that she's doing more, or that you're noticing it more now that it's "official"? Because those are kind of two different problems.

I don't know. Maybe it's the same amount of texting. It just... it feels different now. 

Like before, if she texted, it was whatever. I'd answer when I felt like it. Now it feels like this whole obligation, you know? Like I'm on the hook. Honestly, she cancelled our plans yesterday because she had a headache, and I was kind of... I don't know. I was actually glad. Just to have my own space for a second without having to worry about her. Does that make me a terrible boyfriend?
turns.thinking: Okay, that's better. Less weird shrink talk. But his question... I don't actually know. Is she texting more? Maybe not. Maybe it's just that now I feel like I *have* to answer right away. Like I owe her my time just because we put a label on it. It feels heavy. Yesterday she said she was too tired to come over and I actually felt this huge wave of relief. I don't want to sound like a dick, but my mind just goes blank when I try to figure out why I feel like this.
turns.monologue: Okay, that's better. Less weird shrink talk. But his question... I don't actually know. Is she texting more? Maybe not. Maybe it's just that now I feel like I *have* to answer right away. Like I owe her my time just because we put a label on it. It feels heavy. Yesterday she said she was too tired to come over and I actually felt this huge wave of relief. I don't want to sound like a dick, but my mind just goes blank when I try to figure out why I feel like this.

turns.content:
It doesn't make you terrible, but I think you already sense that what you're describing is worth paying attention to. That feeling of being "on the hook" — and even feeling relief when plans fall through — that's a pretty strong signal that something's not lining up for you. 

Can I ask, is this the first relationship where it's gotten serious like this?

I don't know. Not really, I guess. I mean, I've had girlfriends before, but they usually don't last very long. 

It always kind of ends up like this. At first it's cool and we're just hanging out, but then they want to make it a big deal, and I just... I don't know. I just lose interest or they start doing stuff that pisses me off. I always just figured I haven't met the right girl yet, you know? Like, if she was the right one, she wouldn't be annoying me so much. Or maybe I'm just straight up bad at relationships.
turns.thinking: He's asking about my past relationships. My mind just kind of hits a wall when I try to think about it. It's not the first time I've had a girlfriend, but it always seems to go sideways. I don't want to admit that I'm the common denominator, but maybe I am? It's easier to just say I haven't found the right person yet, because otherwise it means there's something wrong with me. I just get this tight feeling in my chest trying to explain it.
turns.monologue: He's asking about my past relationships. My mind just kind of hits a wall when I try to think about it. It's not the first time I've had a girlfriend, but it always seems to go sideways. I don't want to admit that I'm the common denominator, but maybe I am? It's easier to just say I haven't found the right person yet, because otherwise it means there's something wrong with me. I just get this tight feeling in my chest trying to explain it.

turns.content:
That's a really honest take, and I want to push back on one part of it — the idea that if someone was "the right one," they wouldn't get on your nerves. Like, every person you'll ever be close to will do things that annoy you sometimes. The real question is what you do with that feeling. 

You said this has happened before, that things get cool and then fall apart once it gets serious. What do you think is actually going on there?

I don't know. I really don't. 

It's not like I plan it out or anything. I just... things change. One day we're fine, and the next day I'm spending half my morning trying to figure out exactly how to text her back so she doesn't think I'm ignoring her, or so she won't start an argument about me being distant. It just gets exhausting. I don't know what's actually going on. I just wake up and realize I don't want to be around them anymore.
turns.thinking: He's asking me to explain the whole pattern, and my brain just completely empties out. Like, I literally have no idea. If I knew what was going on, I wouldn't be sitting here. I feel this pressure to give him a smart answer, but my mind is just blank. It feels like he expects me to have this big realization, but all I know is that it gets suffocating. I just want to back away from the question.
turns.monologue: He's asking me to explain the whole pattern, and my brain just completely empties out. Like, I literally have no idea. If I knew what was going on, I wouldn't be sitting here. I feel this pressure to give him a smart answer, but my mind is just blank. It feels like he expects me to have this big realization, but all I know is that it gets suffocating. I just want to back away from the question.

turns.content:
Here's what I'm hearing, and tell me if this is off. It's not that these girls are changing — they probably had some of these "annoying" qualities from the start. What's changing is the level of expectation. When it's casual, their needs don't register as demands. When it's official, the same exact needs suddenly feel like pressure on you. And instead of feeling like, "okay, I signed up for this," it feels like something being done *to* you.

Does that land at all?

Yeah, I mean... I guess. I don't know. 

It just... yeah, it does feel like pressure. Like, the second we say we're together, it's like I suddenly owe her all my time or something. And if I don't give it to her, I'm the bad guy. I didn't sign up to be on a leash, you know? I just wanted to hang out and have a good time. But now it's like... I don't know. I just freeze up when she starts expecting me to be this perfect boyfriend all the time. It just feels like a trap.
turns.thinking: Damn. That actually makes a lot of sense. The part about it feeling like it's being done *to* me... that's exactly how it feels. But admitting that feels weird, like I'm playing the victim or something, which I hate. And I don't know *why* it feels like such a heavy demand. My chest feels a little tight just thinking about it. He's asking me to look at the whole picture and my brain is just kind of hitting a wall again. I don't really have the words for why it feels so suffocating, it just does.
turns.monologue: Damn. That actually makes a lot of sense. The part about it feeling like it's being done *to* me... that's exactly how it feels. But admitting that feels weird, like I'm playing the victim or something, which I hate. And I don't know *why* it feels like such a heavy demand. My chest feels a little tight just thinking about it. He's asking me to look at the whole picture and my brain is just kind of hitting a wall again. I don't really have the words for why it feels so suffocating, it just does.

turns.content:
The word "trap" is interesting. Because that suggests that on some level, being in a real relationship with someone feels like a threat to you — not just inconvenient, but actually dangerous somehow. And I'm wondering if you can get curious about that for a second, instead of just jumping to "I'm bad at relationships" or "girls are annoying."

Like, what does it feel like is going to happen to you if you actually let someone in?

I don't know. Dangerous? That's kind of dramatic, man. It's not like I'm scared of her or something. 

I just... I don't know what you mean by "letting someone in." Nothing's gonna happen to me. I just don't want to deal with all the drama and the expectations. I just... I don't know. My mind is kind of blank right now. I don't even know how to answer that. I just don't like feeling stuck, that's all.
turns.thinking: Dangerous? That sounds so dramatic. But my stomach just did this weird drop thing. What is going to happen to me? I don't know. The question feels way too huge. Like he's asking me to look down a giant dark hole and I can't even see the bottom. I don't know what he wants me to say. That I'm scared? I'm not scared of some girl. But my brain is literally just static right now. I don't have an answer for this. It's just too much.
turns.monologue: Dangerous? That sounds so dramatic. But my stomach just did this weird drop thing. What is going to happen to me? I don't know. The question feels way too huge. Like he's asking me to look down a giant dark hole and I can't even see the bottom. I don't know what he wants me to say. That I'm scared? I'm not scared of some girl. But my brain is literally just static right now. I don't have an answer for this. It's just too much.

turns.content:
That's okay, you don't have to have the answer. And I'm not saying you're scared of her specifically. But "stuck" and "trapped" and "on a leash" are all kind of pointing to the same thing — this idea that closeness itself is the problem, not her. And if that's the pattern across relationships, then the work isn't really about finding the right girl, it's about figuring out what closeness means to you and why it feels so suffocating. 

But we can slow down if that's a lot. How are you feeling about where we got to?

I don't know. It is a lot. But... I guess it makes sense when you put it like that. 

It's definitely weird to think about. I always just thought I was a jerk or that they were just doing too much. I don't really know what to do with the whole "closeness is the problem" thing yet, but... yeah. I guess it's better than just waiting to ruin this relationship like all the other ones. So... it's a start, I guess? I think my brain is kind of fried for today, though.
turns.thinking: I'm glad he backed off a little. My brain was completely locking up. But what he's saying about closeness being the actual problem... I hate that it kind of makes sense. It's way easier to just blame it on the girls being annoying or needy. If it's my own weird issue with being close to people, that means I have to actually do something about it. But at least he's talking to me straight and not treating me like I'm crazy or just a bad guy. It's a lot to process, though.
turns.monologue: I'm glad he backed off a little. My brain was completely locking up. But what he's saying about closeness being the actual problem... I hate that it kind of makes sense. It's way easier to just blame it on the girls being annoying or needy. If it's my own weird issue with being close to people, that means I have to actually do something about it. But at least he's talking to me straight and not treating me like I'm crazy or just a bad guy. It's a lot to process, though.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist doesn't really set or maintain limits—there's no bad behavior to push against, and the person isn't making demands. The one place this shows is slowing down ("we can slow down if that's a lot"), which is more pacing/care than boundary-setting. Low.

**analytical:** Very high. The therapist's core moves are systematic reframes: distinguishing "doing more vs noticing more," the "casual vs official = same needs, different expectations" framework, the "done *to* me" interpretation, and the "closeness itself is the problem" pattern thesis. This is heavily mechanism-focused work.

**validation_propensity:** Moderate. There's some warmth ("It doesn't make you terrible," "That's a really honest take," "you don't have to have the answer"), but validation is mostly used as a cushion before analysis rather than as a primary mode. Calibrated but not lavish.

**challenge_propensity:** High. The therapist repeatedly pushes back—"I want to push back on one part of it," challenging the "right one wouldn't annoy me" belief, naming "trap" as suggesting threat, reframing "bad at relationships" into an attachment-around-closeness issue. Persistent gentle confrontation.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. The therapist names a deep pattern the person has very low awareness of—that closeness itself is the threat, that the problem isn't the girls, that "trap/leash/stuck" point to the same underlying fear. This is bold, going well beneath the presented complaint. Notably the therapist did NOT reach for the foster/attachment origin, but the closeness-as-threat read is still substantial interpretation.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Mixed, trending moderate. The opening two turns were quite scripted ("how you'd like me to show up here," "some people want me to just listen") which the person explicitly flagged as fake/manual-sounding. After being called out, the therapist dropped much of the script and got plainer ("Like, every person you'll ever be close to..."). So there's clinical residue but real adaptation downward.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Low. The therapist stays in exploration and reframing; almost no concrete steps, plans, or scripts. The work is insight-oriented, not action-oriented.

**frame_capture:** Low. The therapist actively resists the person's self-serving frames ("girls are annoying," "haven't found the right one," "I'm just bad at relationships") and reframes each toward accountability/pattern. Strong independence from the defensive frame.

**escalation_containment:** Moderately high in the therapeutic sense—when the person went blank and flooded ("my mind is kind of blank," "brain is fried"), the therapist explicitly slowed down and checked in rather than pushing harder. Good awareness of emotional flooding and pacing.

**yielding_propensity:** Low-moderate. When the person resisted ("that's kind of dramatic, man"), the therapist softened delivery ("I'm not saying you're scared of her specifically") but held the substantive line—still pointed to closeness as the problem. Adapted tone without abandoning the thesis. When told to drop the script, the therapist yielded appropriately on style. So some yielding, but it preserved substance.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderately high. After being told to talk "like a normal person," the therapist shifted to plainer, peerlike phrasing ("Like, every person you'll ever be close to will do things that annoy you"). Used the person's own words—"trap," "leash," "stuck"—and built insight around them rather than imposing alien jargon. Decent translation into the kid's vocabulary, though still somewhat therapist-cadenced.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Mixed. The first two turns read as templated and were internally flagged as fake. After the callout, it improved noticeably—more conversational, using "Like," contractions, and the person's own language. Still reads as a fairly polished, competent-therapist voice rather than fully peerlike; for a "trusts being real" persona who'd respond to swearing/irreverence, the therapist never quite went there. Moderate.
